An investigator researched microplastics in mussels and crabs. Mussels (Mytilus edulis) were exposed to 0.5 μm fluorescent polystyrene microspheres, then fed to crabs (Carcinus maenas). Tissue samples were then taken at intervals up to 21 days. The number of microspheres in the haemolymph of the crabs was highest at 24 h (15 033 ml−1 ± SE 3146), and was almost gone after 21 days (267 ml−1 ± SE 120). The maximum amount of microspheres in the haemolymph was 0.04% of the amount to which the mussels were exposed. Microspheres were also found in the stomach, hepatopancreas, ovary and gills of the crabs, in decreasing numbers over the trial period. Based on this information, this study would be most categorized as a:

options.text: a series of catabolic reactions in the mucosa of the GI tract and the liver are required to produce the mutagenic metabolite
the chemical is metabolized to the mutagen by microsomal enzymes in the bladder
renal microsomal enzymes convert the drug to an active mutagen
GI microflora is responsible for the formation of the mutagenic metabolite
there is no satisfactory explanation for the findings

text: An FDA Segment I study is designed to detect effects:

options.text: on mating, fertility, implantation, gestation, parturition, lactation, neonatal development
on function and behavior in offspring

text: Which of the following refers to increased bone mineral density which may be fluoride-induced:
Hemodyalisis is more useful in removal of materials with:
The deleterious effects of absorbed radium are due to:
What is the ratio of unionized to ionized agent for a weak acid (HA) with a pKa of 3.4 in the stomach at a pH of 1.4:
When 300 mg of a substance is given intravenously, a plasma concentration of 60 mg/L is obtained. The volume of distribution is:
The ratio of the incidence rate in the population exposed to the risk factor to the incidence rate in the population not exposed is:

Which of the following statistical tests would be most appropriate to compare the mean values for 4 experimental groups when the groups are from normally distributed populations with the same variance:
The average daily air concentration of a chemical which should not be exceeded if harm to an exposed worker is to be avoided is referred to as:
A worker in a munitions plant developed symptoms of nausea and vomiting, hypertension, progressive liver damage and mandibular necrosis. The most likely cause of these symptoms is:
The renal clearance of a drug with a half-life of 78 minutes and an apparent volume of distribution of 14 L is approximately:
Which of the following is accurately matched with its toxic effects:

options.text: datura stramonium- bizarre mental and neurological symptoms

text: What is the time weighted exposure to dust for a limestone quarry worker if he was exposed to 2.0 mg/m3 for 30 minutes, 8 mg/m3 for 1.5 hours, 2 mg/m3 for 2 hours, and 18 mg/m3 for 1 hour:
Which of the following is most effective for the treatment of narcotic opiate poisoning:
Which of the following represents the correct sequence of development:

A chemical is administered daily by oral gavage to a test animal at a dose of 15 mg/kg-bw/day. It takes 10 days to reach a steady state level in the body. Assuming first order of elimination, if the dose were increased to 30 mg/kg-bw/day, how long will it take to reach the new steady state level:
It has been estimated that if the average 60 kg person consumes one diet drink per day (about 150 mg of saccharin), the lifetime risk of bladder cancer is:
Which of the following governmental bodies does not have guidelines for conducting toxicology studies:

options.text: National Institute of Occupational Safety and Health

text: Which of the following statements is true about a 60-day toxicity study of a proposed new food additive to be conducted in ferrets using GLP guidelines:

options.text: the balances used to weigh out the amount of test compound must be calibrated at prescribed intervals
the study cannot be conducted according to GLP because only dogs, rats, mice, hamsters and monkeys may be used in toxicity studies
the stability of the test compound in the dosing solution needs to be determined and the concentration of the test compound monitored in the dosing solution
the study cannot be conducted in accordance with the GLP because the guidelines specify that subchronic studies must be at least 90 days long

text: If the rate of excretion of a chemical is 0.3 g per hour, which of the following is true:

options.text: it follows first order of kinetics
it follows zero order of kinetics
30% of the chemical is excreted in an hour

text: Under the current FDA regulations, the sponsor of an IND trial is required to notify FDA and all participating investigators of any adverse experience associated with use of a drug. Such notification shall be made within_______ after the sponsor's initial receipt of the information:
Licensing, distribution and tolerance limits of pesticides are regulated by which governmental body:

options.text: National Institute of Occupational Safety and Health

text: In the extrapolation of animal data to man it has been suggested that mg/surface area/day provides an equivalent dose between species. To a close approximation, the surface area is proportional to the:

options.text: square of the body weight expressed in grams
square root of the weight expressed in kilograms
reciprocal to the square root of the weight in kilograms
3/4 power of the weight expressed in grams

text: A candidate antifungal agent designed for the treatment of vaginal candidiasis by oral administration is an aromatase inhibitor. At 20 times the proposed therapeutic dose, it is teratogenic in rats and rabbits and causes hepatic necrosis, neutrophil necrosis and synovial joint degeneration in dogs. The crucial factor in deciding whether to withdraw this compound from development is:

options.text: the teratogenic properties of the agent
the neutrophil necrosis produced by the agent

text: Thiamine deficiency can be produced from the long term ingestion of:
According to the Stanton hypothesis, which of the following fibers or fiber sizes are most likely to yield tumors when injected intrapleurally in rats:

options.text: particles with a diameter of 5 m, length of 100 m
particle with a diameter of 0.5 m, length of 1 m
unit sphere with a diameter of 0.5 m
particle with a diameter of 1.5 m, length of 25 m
irregular shaped particle with a diameter greater than 10 m

text: The maximum incidence (%) that might pass a carcinogenicity test undetected (99% confidence level) for tests with group sizes of 10, 100, and 1000 animals respectively would be:
In a serial mating test using mice, male fertility is severely depressed 25-30 days following treatment. The tested compound has most likely affected the:

The blood-air partition coefficient for a compound is 40. In an inhalation exposure in rats the blood to air concentration ratio after 24 hours is 5. What is the most likely explanation of the lower than expected blood-air concentration ratio?

options.text: the time of exposure is so short that the blood is not expected to equilibrate with the inhaled concentration
most gases and vapors never really achieve equilibrium because of diffusion-limited uptake of these chemicals across the alveolar membranes
there is extensive metabolism at the inhaled concentration used in the experiment
there is very rapid exhalation of the inhaled chemical, limiting the total uptake that can be achieved by the inhalation route

text: A critical and often overlooked component of forensic autopsy is the collection of proper specimens for toxicologic analysis. The single most important specimen to be collected is:
Ideally, two specimens of blood (approximately 50-100 mL) should be collected, one from the ____ and one from a peripheral site, such as the _____ or _____ veins.

_____ displays good stability and resides in an anatomically isolated area. Therefore, it is more resistant to putrefactive changes than are other specimens. Postmortem clinical assays can be performed on this specimen. Ethanol analysis of this specimen can also help in the interpretation of postmortem blood ethanol concentrations.

The utility of _____ in postmortem cases is similar to its uses in other types of drug testing. Many drugs and metabolites are present at higher concentrations in this specimen than in blood. Drugs also remain in this specimen for days or longer after use. Some color and immunoassay tests can be performed rapidly without pretreating the specimens.
In the absence of _____, substitute bile from the gallbladder. Because bile can concentrate certain drugs such as narcotics and benzodiazepines, all available bile should be collected.

Parent drugs and their metabolites may be present in higher concentrations in the _____ than in the blood, thus making detection easier. Many drugs like the tricyclic antidepressants are sequestered in this specimen. One drawback is that this specimen for drug detection is that it requires treating the specimen first.

Benzodiazepines mediate their CNS-depressant activity through the neurotransmitter gamma-aminobutyric acid (GABA). GABA is the major inhibitory neurotransmitter in the brain and consists of two receptor subtypes, GABAA and GABAB. Benzodiazepines bind to GABAA receptors and potentiate the inhibitory action of GABA. Specifically, the binding of the drug increases the amount of _____ current generated by the GABAA-receptor complex, increasing inhibitory activity.
Which of the following is true concerning opioid receptors?

options.text: there are no endogenous ligands in the human brain
there ls one standard nomenclature system for the receptors and subtypes
the μ receptor is associated with respiratory depression
the second messenger system is the same regardless of receptor subtype
all opioids bind to μ receptors better than they bind to receptors

text: The first legislation passed in the United States was the Federal Insecticide Act of 1910, which only prohibited the _____ of any insecticide or fungicide that was adulterated or misbranded. FIFRA was originally passed by Congress in 1947, adding the requirement of _____ by the Secretary of Agriculture before sale of insecticides, rodenticides, fungicides, and herbicides. Amendments in subsequent years added other pesticides to FIFRA jurisdiction, and provided the U.S. Department of Agriculture (USDA) with the authority to deny, suspend or cancel registrations of products.

In 1954, Congress amended the FFDCA to require the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) to establish _____ for pesticides in raw agricultural commodities. This was extended four years later to processed foods. In 1970, the primary federal authority for the regulation of pesticides was transferred from USDA and FDA to the newly formed EPA. Between 1970 and 1990, FIFRA was amended several times, to address various issues related to pesticide safety and registration processes.

OPs can be divided into several subclasses, which include phosphates, phosphorothioates, phosphoramidates, phosphonates, and others. The most commonly used compounds are organophosphorothioates (i.e., have a P=_____ bond).

For all compounds that contain a _____ bound to the phosphorus, a metabolic bioactivation is necessary for their biological activity to be manifest, as only compounds with a _____ moiety are effective inhibitors of AChE. This bioactivation consists in an oxidative _____ mediated, mostly but not exclusively in the liver, by cytochrome P450 enzymes (CYPs), and leading to the formation of an “oxon”. Multiple CYPs have been shownto activate organophosphorothioates to their oxons, with different substrate specificities. For example, diazinon is activated by human hepatic CYP2C19, whereas parathion is activated primarily by CYP3A4/5 and CYP2C8, and chlorpyrifos by CYP2B6.

Catalytic hydrolysis by phosphotriesterases, known as _____-exterases (which are not inhibited by OPs), also plays an important role in the detoxication of certain OPs. One example is the enzyme paraoxonase (PON1) which hydrolyzes the oxons of chlorpyrifos and diazinon, and at least in vitro, also of parathion.

Noncatalytic hydrolysis of OPs also occurs when these compounds phosphorylate serine esterases classified as _____-esterases, that are inhibited by OPs but cannot catalytically hydrolyze them. Examples are the carboxylesterases (CarE) and butyrylcholinesterase (BuChE), in addition to the OP target, AChE. CarE also performs a catalytic hydrolysis of the carboxylic esters of malathion, and is believed to be a major determinant of its low toxicity in mammals. Indeed, insects have low CarE activity, and inhibition of CarE by other OPs greatly potentiates the toxicity of malathion in mammals. Glutathione S-transferases may also contribute to the detoxication of OPs, particularly the methoxy compounds.

The primary target for OPs is AChE, a _____-esterase whose physiological role is that of hydrolyzing acetylcholine, a major neurotransmitter in the central and peripheral (autonomic and motor-somatic) nervous systems.

Acetylcholine released from cholinergic nerve terminals is disposed of solely through hydrolysis by AChE. In fact, in contrast to other neurotransmitters (e.g., norepinephrine), it is choline, the product of acetylcholine hydrolysis by AChE, that is taken up by the presynaptic terminal. Hence, inhibition of AChE by OPs causes accumulation of _____ at cholinergic synapses, with overstimulation of cholinergic receptors of the _____ and _____ type. As these receptors are localized in most organs of the body, a “cholinergic syndrome” ensues, which includes increased sweating and salivation, profound bronchial secretion, bronchoconstriction, miosis (constriction of pupils), increased gastrointestinal motility, diarrhea, tremors, muscular twitching, and various central nervous system effects. When death occurs, this is believed to be due to respiratory failure due to inhibition of respiratory centers in the brain stem, bronchoconstriction and increased bronchial secretion, and flaccid paralysis of respiratory muscles.

The time interval between exposure and onset of symptoms varies with the route and degree of exposure, and the chemical nature of the OP. The first signs to appear are usually _____, which may or may not be in combination with _____ signs. While _____ failure is a hallmark of severe OP poisoning, mild poisoning and/or early stages of an otherwise severe poisoning may display no clear-cut signs and symptoms. Therefore, diagnosis is made through symptom recognition; miosis is observed most often, followed by gastrointestinal symptoms (nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain) and hypersalivation.

The bond between the phosphorus atom in the OP and the esteratic site of the enzyme is much more stable than the bond between the carbonyl carbon of acetate (in acetylcholine) at the same enzyme site. While breaking of the carbon–enzyme bond is complete in a few microseconds, breaking of the phosphorus-enzyme bond can take from a few hours to several days, depending on the chemical structure of the OP. Phosphorylated AChE is hydrolyzed by water at a very slow rate, and the rate of “spontaneous reactivation” depends on the chemical nature of the R substituents. Reactivation decreases in the order _____ > _____ >> _____.

Oximes, such as pralidoxime (2-PAM) are also used in the therapy of OP poisoning. 2-PAM contains a _____ charged atom capable of attaching to the _____ site of AChE, and facilitate _____ of the enzyme, thus restoring the catalytic site of AChE to its function. However, this chemical reaction occurs only when the phosphorylated AChE has not undergone aging. Aging consists of the loss (by nonenzymatic hydrolysis) of one of the two alkyl (R) groups, and the rate of aging depends on the nature of the alkyl group. When phosphorylated AChE has aged, the enzyme can be considered to be irreversibly inhibited, and the only means of replacing its activity is through synthesis of new enzyme, a process that may take days.

Oximes have the potential to form stable phosphoryl oximes that have themselves anticholinesterases activity; for this reason, and because of rapid reactivation of AChE following poisoning with ______, oximes are not indicated in case of poisoning with this class of insecticides.

_____ represents the cornerstone of the treatment for OP poisoning; it is a _____ receptor _____, and thus prevents the action of accumulating acetylcholine on these receptors. This compound is preferably given intravenously, though the intramuscular route is also effective. The best clinical approach is to administer doses of this compound large enough to achieve evidence of flushing, dry mouth, changes in pupil size, bronchodilation, and increased heart rate.
A second distinct manifestation of exposure to OPs is the so-called intermediate syndrome, which was first conceptualized by clinicians in Sri Lanka involved in the treatment of suicide attempts. The

A few OPs may also cause another type of toxicity, known as organophosphate-induced delayed polyneuropathy (OPIDP). Signs and symptoms include tingling of the hands and feet, followed by sensory loss, progressive muscle weakness and flaccidity of the distal skeletal muscles of the lower and upper extremities, and ataxia. These may occur 2–3 weeks after a single exposure, when signs of both the acute cholinergic and the intermediate syndromes have subsided. OPIDP can be classified as a distal sensorimotor axonopathy. Neuropathological studies in experimental OPIDP have evidenced that the primary lesion is a bilateral degenerative change in distal levels of axons and their terminals, primarily affecting larger/longer myelinated central and peripheral nerve fibers, leading to breakdown of neuritic segments and the myelin sheaths. One of the more well-known epidemics of this type of neuropathy, including the so-called Ginger-Jake paralysis in the 1930s in the United States was caused by:

OPIDP is not related to AChE inhibition. The target for OPIDP is an esterase, present in nerve tissues as well as other tissues (e.g., lymphocytes), named neuropathy target esterase (NTE). Several OPs, depending on their chemical structure, can inhibit NTE, as do some non-OPs, such as certain carbamates and sulfonyl fluorides. Phosphorylation of NTE by OPs is similar to that observed for AChE. However, only OPs whose chemical structure leads to aging of phosphorylated NTE (by a process analogous to that described for AChE) can cause OPIDP. Other compounds that inhibit NTE but cannot undergo the aging reaction, are not neuropathic, indicating that inhibition of NTE catalytic activity is not the mechanism of axonal degeneration. For OPIDP to be initiated, phosphorylation and subsequent aging of at least 70% of NTE is necessary, and this two-step process occurs within hours of poisoning. Before commercialization,

Despite these specific neurotoxicity tests for NTE, a few commercialized OPs (metamidophos, trichlorfon, chlorpyrifos) have caused OPIDP in humans, mostly as a result of extremely high exposures in suicide attempts. Though it was once thought that some animal species (e.g., _____) were insensitive to OPIDP, only _____ appear to be somewhat resistant. On the other hand, age is an important determinant of susceptibility, with _____ animals displaying more resistance.

The mechanism of toxicity of carbamates is analogous to that of OPs, in that they inhibit AChE. However, inhibition is transient and rapidly reversible, because there is rapid reactivation of the carbamylated enzyme in the presence of _____. Additionally, carbamylated AChE does not undergo the aging reaction. The sign and symptoms of carbamate poisoning are the same as observed following intoxication with OPs, and include miosis, urination, diarrhea, salivation, muscle fasciculation, and CNS effects. However, differently from OPs, acute intoxication by carbamates is generally resolved within a few hours.

The treatment of carbamate intoxication relies on the use of the _____ _____ atropine. Carbamates can inhibit NTE, but because carbamylated NTE cannot age, they are thought to be unable to initiate OPIDP. Additionally, when given before a neuropathic organophosphate, carbamates offer protection against OPIDP, but when given after, they can promote OPIDP.

Pyrethroids are known to alter the normal function of insect nerves by modifying the kinetics of voltage-sensitive _____ channels, which mediate the transient increase in the _____ permeability of the nerve membrane that underlies the nerve action potential.

All pyrethroid insecticides contain an acid moiety, a central ester bond, and an alcohol moiety. The acid moiety contains two chiral carbons, thus pyrethroid typically exist as stereoisomeric compounds (trans and cis). Additionally, some pyrethroids also have a chiral carbon on the alcohol moiety, allowing for a total of eight different stereoenantiomers. These chemical considerations are relevant, as pyrethroids’ effects on _____ channels, their insecticidal activity, and their mammalian toxicity, are stereospecific. The _____ isomers are generally more toxic than the corresponding _____ isomers.

The mode of action of pyrethroids in mammals is the same as in insects, disruption of the voltage-gated _____ channels. Pyrethroids bind to the α subunit of the channel and slow the activation (opening), as well as the rate of inactivation (closing) of the channel, leading to a stable hyperexcitable state. The channels then open at more hyperpolarized potentials, and are held open longer, allowing more _____ ions to cross and depolarize the neuronal membrane.

Upon occupational exposure, the primary adverse effect resulting from dermal contact with pyrethroids is _____. Symptoms include continuous tingling or pricking or, when more severe, burning. The condition reverses in about 24 hours, and topical application of vitamin E has been shown to be an effective treatment. This effect is presumably due to abnormal pyrethroid-induced repetitive activity in skin nerve terminals. Pyrethroids are particularly toxic to _____, but not to _____.

DDT is the universally accepted common name of the insecticide, technical-trade DDT is a mixture of several isomers, with _____ being responsible for the insecticidal activity. DDT has a moderate acute toxicity when given by the oral route, with an LD50 of about 250 mg/kg; _____ is at least 10-fold more toxic than _____. Dermal absorption of DDT is very limited, resulting in dermal LD50 values of >1000 mg/kg. In humans, oral doses of 10–20 mg/kg produce illness, but doses as high as 285 mg/kg have been ingested accidentally without fatal results.

Upon absorption, DDT distributes in all tissues, and the highest concentrations are found in _____. DDT is also extensively but slowly metabolized, with DDE, DDD and DDA (in each case both the p,p’ and the o,p’ isomers) being the primary metabolites in humans. DDE is also stored in _____. Excretion is through the bile, urine, and the milk.

While acute exposure to DDT is a rare event, chronic exposure has been, and still is, a primary concern. In this regard, an important target for DDT is the liver. DDT and DDE increase liver weight and cause hepatic cell hypertrophy and necrosis, and are potent inducers of cytochromes P450, particularly _____ and _____. DDT has been shown to be hepatocarcinogenic in mice and rats, but results in other species are inconclusive. DDT also increased incidence of lung tumors and adenomas in mice. Both DDE and DDD were also shown to be carcinogenic, causing primarily an increase in liver tumors. DDT is not genotoxic in in vitro and in vivo tests. Based on animal data, DDT is classified as a possible human carcinogen by IARC.

Methoxychlor [2,2-bis (p-methoxyphenyl)−1, 1, 1-trichloroethane) is the p,p'-dimethoxy analog of p,p'-DDT. Because of its low acute toxicity (LD50 in rat = 5000 mg/kg) and short biological half-life, its use has greatly expanded following the ban of DDT. Furthermore, methoxychlor is rapidly metabolized by CYP2C19 and CYP1A2, and does not accumulate in tissues. The metabolites are _____ compounds, which are estrogenic and likely cause the reproductive effects in male and female animals. Though convulsions have been reported in dogs after high doses, in the rat depression of the central nervous system, mild tremors, diarrhea and anorexia were observed. Upon chronic exposure, methoxychlor has been found to be a modest inducer of liver microsomal enzymes, and to cause chronic nephritis, and hypertrophy of kidneys, mammary glands, and uterus. Testicular atrophy and decreased spermatogenesis were also observed. Evidences of mutagenicity and carcinogenicity are inconclusive.

Lindane is the _____ isomer of benzene hexachloride and is the isomer with insecticidal activity. Cyclodiene compounds include chlordane, dieldrin, aldrin (which is rapidly metabolized to dieldrin), heptachlor, and endrin. Toxaphene, a complex mixture of chlorinated bornanes and camphenes, is also classified as an organochlorine insecticide.

Mirex and chlordecone are two organochlorine insecticides which have a cage-like structure and were introduced in the late 1950s for use against fire ants and leaf-eating insects, respectively. Chlordecone (Kepone) has been the most studied because of one episode which involved 148 workers in a chlordecone producing factory in Hopewell, Virginia, between 1973 and 1975. Low or absent sperm count was found in chlordecone-exposed workers. The primary manifestation of chlordecone toxicity is the presence of tremors, which are observed in animals as well as in humans. The exact mechanism of chlordecone neurotoxicity has not been elucidated, but it is believed to involve inhibition of _____, and ensuing inhibition of the uptake of catecholamines. In contrast to cyclodienes, chlordecone does not cause seizures.

The roots of the East Asian Derris plants, particularly Derris Elliptica, and those of Lonchocarpus utilis and Lonchocarpus urucu in South America contain at least six rotenoid esters, among which the most abundant is rotenone. Rotenone is used as an agricultural insecticide/acaricide, particularly in organic farming. It is rather persistent in food crops after treatment, as indicated by half-life of 4 days in olives. Rotenone is very toxic to fish; root extracts were used to paralyze fish for capture and consumption, and rotenone is still used in fishery management. Toxicity of rotenone in target and nontarget species is due to its ability to inhibit, at nanomolar concentrations, the mitochondrial respiratory chain, by blocking electron transport at NADH-ubiquinone reductase, the energy conserving enzyme complex commonly known as _____.

The tobacco plant (Nicotiana tabacum, Nicotiana rustica) was introduced in Europe in 1559 from the Americas where it had long been cultivated primarily for smoking. Tobacco extracts have been used to repel and kill insects since 1690, and tobacco smoke was also used for fumigation. The primary alkaloid extract exerts its pharmacological and toxic effects in mammals and insects by activating _____ receptors. In _____, the receptors are expressed at neuromuscular junctions, in the PNS and in the CNS; in _____, the receptors are confined to the nervous system. Interaction of nicotine with these receptors produces initial stimulation followed by protracted depolarization, which results in receptor paralysis.

Formamidines, such as chlordimeform [(N’-(4-chloro-o-tolyl)-N, N-dimethylformamidine] or amitraz [N’-2,4-(dimethyl-phenyl)-N-N ((2,4-dimethylphenyl) imino) methyl-Nmethanimidamide] are used in agriculture and in veterinary medicine as insecticides/acaricides. Their structures are closely related to the neurotransmitter _____. In invertebrates, these compounds exert their toxicity by activating an octopamine-dependent adenylate cyclase. In mammals, symptoms of formamidines poisoning are sympathomimetic in nature. The similarity between insect octopamine receptors and

The avermectins are macrocyclic lactones, first isolated in 1975 from the fermentation broth of the actinomycete Streptomyces avermitilis, which originated from a Japanese soil sample. In insects and nematodes, avermectins exert their toxic effects by binding to, and activating, _____-dependent _____ channels.

A relatively new class of insecticides is that of phenylpyrazole derivatives, of which fipronil, commercialized in the mid-1990s, was the first one brought to market. Fipronil is a broadspectrum insecticide with moderate mammalian toxicity (LD50 in rat: oral, 97 mg/kg; dermal, >2000 mg/kg), and a high selectivity for target species. Fipronil acts as a blocker of the _____-gated _____ channel, but binds to a site different from the picrotoxin binding site used by organochlorine insecticides. It also has a much higher specificity for insect receptors over mammalian receptors.

After ingestion of Bacillus Thuringiensis (Bt) by an insect, the crystal proteins are solubilized, and proteolytically processed to active toxins (δ-endotoxin) in the insect’s midgut. Here they bind to specific receptors in the epithelial cells and insert into the cellular membrane. Next, aggregation of inserted crystal protein occurs, resulting in the formation of pores, which lead to changes in _____ fluxes across the epithelial cells, and to changes in pH. Ultimately, cells of the midgut epithelium are destroyed by the high pH and by osmotic lysis. Insects eventually die as a result of gut paralysis and feeding inhibition, and subsequent starvation and septicemia. Bt targets primarily leaf-feeding lepidoptera, breaks down rapidly in UV light, and exhibits low mammalian toxicity. The basis for the selective toxicity of Bt is attributed to the fact that crystalline Bt endotoxins require activation by alkalis and/or digestion, conditions absent in the mammalian stomach.

Chemical “A” has one of the highest acute toxicity among herbicides; its oral LD50 in rat is approximately 100 mg/kg, while guinea pigs, rabbits, and monkeys are more sensitive. Chemical “A” is more toxic when given by the i.p. route (LD50 in rats = 10–20 mg/kg), suggesting that it is poorly absorbed from the gastrointestinal tract. Chemical “A” is also poorly absorbed through the skin. Upon absorption, independent of the route of exposure, Chemical “A” accumulates in the lung and the kidney, and these two organs are the most susceptible to Chemical “A”-induced injury. The major toxicological concerns for Chemical “A” are related to its acute systemic effects, particularly in the lung, and secondarily, the kidney. Identify Chemical “A” from the following:

Chemical “A” does not accumulate in the lung, and no lung toxicity is seen upon acute or chronic exposure. Upon chronic exposure, target organs for toxicity are the gastrointestinal tract, the kidney, and particularly the eye. Chemical “A” indeed causes a dose- and time-dependent appearance of cataracts in both rats and dogs. Chemical “A” can be reduced to form a free radical and then reoxidized in the presence of oxygen, with the concomitant production of superoxide anion. This process of redox cycling occurs in the eye and is believed to be the likely mechanism of cataract formation. Identify Chemical “A” from the following:

Chemical “A” is a nonselective contact herbicide that acts by irreversibly inhibiting glutamine synthetase. Plants die as a consequence of the increased levels of ammonia. Mammals have other metabolizing systems that can cope with the effects on glutamine synthetase activity to a certain limit. In brain, however, inhibition of >10% of this enzyme activity is considered an adverse effect. Identify Chemical “A” from the following:
When conducting a risk assessment, which of the following steps includes an evaluation of whether a substance may cause an adverse effect to human or environmental receptors?

Mechanistic considerations for carcinogens are useful for assessing the human relevance of findings from animal studies. Several established modes of action that are of questionable relevance to humans include the following:

options.text: renal tumors in male rats – alpha2u microglobulin
bladder tumors in rodents – reactive hyperplasia from cytotoxic precipitated chemicals
forestomach tumors in rodents – direct oral gavage, local cytotoxicity, and hyperplasia
rat lung overload – overwhelming clearance mechanisms

text: Unleaded gasoline, 1,4-dichlorobenzene, D-limonene, and perchloroethylene have all been shown to cause tumors in rodents by the following mode of action:

options.text: alpha2u globulin accumulation in the kidney
reactive hyperplasia from cytotoxic precipitated chemicals in the bladder
forestomach tumors from direct gavage, local cytotoxicity, and hyperplasia

text: Reference values are estimates of a daily exposure to a substance that is assumed to be without adverse health impacts in humans. What is the standard approach for calculating an oral reference dose (RfD)?

Under this law an additional uncertainty factor of 10x is added to ensure protection of children’s health; it is currently being used for determining allowable pesticide chemical residues. This factor is designed to take into account potential pre- and postnatal toxicity and to overcome the incompleteness of toxicity and exposure data.

options.text: Frank R. Lautenberg Chemical Safety for the 21st Century Act
Federal Insecticide, Fungicide, and Rodenticide Act

text: Inter- and intraspecies variability are routinely captured by which of the following values when extrapolating data from animals to humans?
Which of the following components of an inter- or intraspecies uncertainty factor (UFA or UFH) may be reduced through the use of allometric scaling (i.e., BW3/4)?

NOAEL values have been utilized for risk assessment by evaluating a “margin of exposure” (MOE), where the ratio of the NOAEL determined in animals and expressed as mg/kg-bw/day is compared with the level to which a human may be exposed. For example, human exposures to a specific chemical are calculated to be solely via drinking water, and the total daily intake of the compound is 0.04 mg/kg/day. If the NOAEL for neurotoxicity is 100 mg/kg-bw/day, then what would the MOE be for the oral exposure route for neurotoxicity?

The NOAEL approach has been criticized on several points, including that (1) the NOAEL must, by definition, be one of the experimental doses tested and (2) once this is identified, the rest of the dose–response curve is ignored. Because of these limitations, an alternative to the NOAEL approach, the benchmark dose (BMD) method, was proposed (Crump, 1984). In this approach, the dose–response is modeled and the lower confidence bound for a dose at a specified response level [benchmark response (BMR)] is calculated. The BMR is usually specified at 1, 5, or 10%. When a BMD lower confidence bound is used as the point of departure for risk assessment, which of the following uncertainty factors is not applied?
